Triggers.value: When people say things like my ex

Threat.value: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Negative Appraisals.value: My reactions since the event show that I am a lousy coper
I feel isolated and set apart from others
I can't trust that I will do the right thing

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I try to push them out of my mind
Watching TV programmes/ films or reading about similar events

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.
When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.

issues.explanation: Age 77 is outside the valid range of 18-80

issues.item: Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.
issues.explanation: Direct contradiction within Memory node: 'Verbal Access' (easy verbal recall) conflicts with 'Reliving Event' (intense re-experiencing) and 'Vivid Memory' (detailed recall) - patients with severe re-experiencing typically struggle with verbalization due to emotional overwhelm.

issues.item: Memory Erasure: I try to erase the memory of the event
issues.explanation: Inconsistent with Memory node: Actively trying to erase memories contradicts having 'Vivid Memory' and 'Verbal Access' - patients with clear, easily verbalized memories typically don't report active erasure attempts.

Mismatch with PCL-5 severity: With PCL-5 score of 43 (severe PTSD), the symptom profile is too mild - only one cognitive symptom (concentration) alongside vivid memories and easy verbalization doesn't align with severe impairment.

vignette:
**Clinical Vignette**

Mr. R., a 35-year-old divorced man from South America, presents with significant distress following his military service. His life is now punctuated by moments of intense threat. A cold draft, a particular song on the radio, or an offhand mention of an ex-partner can instantly trigger a state of high alert. He becomes hypervigilant, his body reacting with a powerful startle response, as if the original danger were present. These triggers do more than just provoke fear; they also reinforce his most painful beliefs about himself. In the wake of such reactions, he is left feeling like a "lousy coper," isolated from a world that seems to move on without him, and plagued by self-doubt about his own judgment.

The traumatic events themselves remain a source of profound disturbance. His memories are not just thoughts but vivid, sensory-laden relivings that feel as if they are happening in the present moment. He finds these experiences impossible to articulate, trapped in a cycle of nonverbal recollection. This fragmented and overwhelming memory directly fuels his negative self-appraisals, convincing him that his reactions are proof of a fundamental personal failing.

To manage the pervasive sense of threat and the painful beliefs it generates, Mr. R. has adopted several maladaptive strategies. He drinks alcohol to numb his feelings and deliberately avoids television programmes or films that depict military conflict. When intrusive memories surface, he makes a concerted effort to push them out of his mind. His PCL-5 score of 43 indicates a clinically significant level of post-traumatic stress.

Ultimately, his world has become smaller and more perilous. The constant cycling between external triggers, internalized threat, harsh self-critique, and ineffective coping strategies maintains his distress. He is caught in a pattern where efforts to control his fear and shame only serve to reinforce his sense of isolation and inadequacy, leaving him disconnected from the support and stability he needs.
